Plaintiff_Demographics: State prisoner, pro se plaintiff; age and sex not specified.

Case_Name: Nilda TORRES NIEVES, et al., Plaintiffs, v. HOSPITAL METROPOLITANO, et al., Defendants, v. Dr. Dimas FERRER, Third Party Defendant.
Court: United States District Court, D. Puerto Rico.
Expert_Criticism_Text: Defense expert Dr. Calimano testified that the plaintiff received excellent care, appropriate screening and stabilization; plaintiff's own expert admitted only a rectal exam was omitted and that it would not have changed the diagnosis.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendants argued that the plaintiff received adequate medical screening and was stabilized before transfer, satisfying EMTALA, and that there was no breach of the standard of care or causal link between any alleged negligence and the ureter injury that occurred during surgery at the receiving hospital.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: inadequate medical screening, failure to stabilize before transfer, medical malpractice
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Ureter laceration was discovered after the appendectomy when imaging was performed post‑operatively.
Operative_Text_Snippet: Appendectomy was performed by surgeons at around 6:00 p.m. on January 16, 1992.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: Ureter was nicked during the appendectomy; a subsequent CT‑Scan revealed the injury.
Plaintiff_Demographics: UNKNOWN
Reviewer_Notes: The opinion provides detailed legal analysis but limited clinical detail; many procedure‑specific fields are unclear or not mentioned.

Case_Name: Ronald W. YOUNG, Appellant, v. Frederick C. FISHBACK, Appellee.
Court: United States Court of Appeals District of Columbia Circuit.
Expert_Criticism_Text: The trial judge erred in directing a verdict because the plaintiffs had not produced expert testimony to show that leaving gauze threads was a departure from the standard of care.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: The defense moved for a directed verdict, arguing that without expert testimony there was no proof of negligence and that the alleged foreign body did not constitute a breach of surgical standards.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: foreign body left in wound, negligent performance of appendectomy
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Abscess was discovered when the surgeon reopened the wound to relieve it.
Operative_Text_Snippet: About a year after the appellee, Dr. Fishback, performed an appendectomy on Ronald W. Young, the appellant, an abscess the size of a small egg had developed on the scar. After Dr. Fishback operated again to relieve the abscess...
Recognition_Text_Snippet: The plaintiffs alleged that a foreign body had been left in the wound at the time of the first operation and had caused the abscess.
Plaintiff_Demographics: UNKNOWN
Preventability_Assessment: The court suggested that the presence of a foreign body could have been avoided with proper surgical technique, implying preventability.
Reviewer_Notes: Many clinical details such as procedure approach, disease state, and timing metrics are not provided; custody status and claim type are inferred from context.

Case_Name: Yoon Kyeong LEE and Seung Ho Son, Plaintiffs, v. ABINGTON MEMORIAL HOSPITAL, Valerie Dyke, M.D., Robert K. Josloff, M.D., and Surgical Care Specialists, Inc., Defendants
Court: United States District Court, E.D. Pennsylvania
Expert_Criticism_Text: Plaintiff's expert Dr. Richard H. Pearl criticized the hospital's policy of treating uninsured patients by resident physicians, calling the practice outrageous and a violation of established procedures.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: The hospital moves for summary judgment on punitive damages, arguing plaintiffs have not produced evidence of evil motive or reckless indifference.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: failure to follow established rules and regulations, two-tiered care policy for uninsured patients, failure to inform patients of resident vs attending physicians, alleged outrageous conduct
Recognition_Timing_Detail: During exploratory surgery, Dr. Dyke discovered a four millimeter perforation of the sigmoid colon.
Operative_Text_Snippet: Dr. Dyke performed the appendectomy at approximately 2:45 a.m. on August 8, 1997, approximately six hours after Ms. Lee’s arrival at Abington Hospital.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: During the exploratory surgery, Dr. Dyke discovered a four millimeter perforation of the sigmoid colon.
Plaintiff_Demographics: Ms. Lee, female patient admitted August 7, 1997; husband Seung Ho Son; no age provided.
Surgeon_Characteristics: Dr. Valerie Dyke, fifth-year surgical resident; Dr. Robert Josloff, staff surgeon.
Reviewer_Notes: Opinion provides limited clinical detail; many fields lack explicit information.

Case_Name: Richard ARMENTA, Plaintiff, v. SHAH, et al., Defendants.
Court: United States District Court, E.D. California.
Procedural_Posture: Screening order granting in forma pauperis and denying counsel motion

Recognition_Timing_Detail: Plaintiff was told three days after surgery that the appendix was not found; ten days later a doctor confirmed no appendix was identified in the tissue removed.
Operative_Text_Snippet: the post-surgical report, completed by defendant Shah, detailed the successful removal of plaintiff’s appendix.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: he thought he removed plaintiff’s appendix, but it was tissue… there was “NO APPENDIX IDENTIFIED” in the tissue removed during plaintiff’s surgery.
Plaintiff_Demographics: state prisoner; male; age not specified

Case_Name: John David HAMRAC, Plaintiff v. Dr. Craig B. MIZES; St. Mary’s Physicians Services, LLC, Defendants
Court: United States District Court, E.D. Arkansas, Central Division.
Procedural_Posture: Defendants' motions for partial and summary judgment granted
Expert_Criticism_Text: Plaintiff's expert Dr. Awad testified that low hemoglobin indicated ongoing bleed; Defendant's toxicology expert testified no evidence of intoxication and drug screens were negative.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendants argued no negligence in the surgery, no gross negligence, and that the plaintiff's claims were unsupported, seeking summary judgment which was granted.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: negligent performance of appendectomy, negligent retention, negligent supervision, gross negligence, premature discharge
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Diagnostic laparoscopy performed on August 12 revealed old blood from the internal bleed that occurred after the appendectomy.
Operative_Text_Snippet: Dr. Craig Mizes performed a laparoscopic appendectomy on Plaintiff John David Hamrac.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: Plaintiff underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy at which time old blood from the internal bleed that had occurred following the appendectomy was evacuated.
Plaintiff_Demographics: John David Hamrac, adult male, on vacation in Arkansas, Medicaid insurance.
Surgeon_Characteristics: Dr. Craig B. Mizes, surgeon, license suspended in 2014 for opioid use.
Facility_Type: Hospital (St. Mary’s Physicians Services) and West Florida Hospital.
Preventability_Assessment: Court found no evidence of negligence; claims dismissed, indicating the injuries were not deemed preventable under the standard of care.

Case_Name: TANCRELL v. FREEPORT MEMORIAL HOSPITAL, INC., et al.
Court: United States District Court, N.D. Illinois.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendants argued that Dr. McGlone was not an employee or agent of the hospital and that the consent form did not clearly notify the plaintiff of his independent contractor status, thus no apparent agency liability.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: negligent performance of appendectomy, failure to remove all appendix tissue, failure to establish apparent agency
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Plaintiff experienced abdominal pain in 2003 and underwent another surgery, alleging it was due to the original failure to remove all appendix tissue.
Operative_Text_Snippet: Dr. McGlone proceeded to perform a laparoscopic appendectomy on plaintiff on October 27, 2001. After the surgery, Dr. McGlone did not rely on any pathology reports to determine whether he had successfully removed the entire appendix.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: plaintiff had abdominal pain and presented for treatment at Medical Associates Clinic in Dubuque, Iowa. In his complaint, plaintiff alleges that this abdominal pain, along with another surgery to remove his appendix, were due to Dr. McGlone’s failure to remove all of plaintiff’s appendix during the appendectomy on October 27, 2001.
Plaintiff_Demographics: UNKNOWN
Surgeon_Characteristics: Dr. McGlone, independent contractor surgeon, sole proprietor

Case_Name: Andrew Cleveland ROGERS, III and Andrew Cleveland Rogers, Jr., Plaintiffs-Appellants, v. UNITED STATES of America, Defendant-Appellee.
Court: United States Court of Appeals Sixth Circuit
Expert_Criticism_Text: The court noted conflicting medical testimony and found the plaintiffs failed to prove negligence, indicating the expert evidence was insufficient to establish a breach of the standard of care.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: The government argued that the physicians acted within reasonable medical judgment, that antibiotics would have been ineffective against the infecting organism, and that there was no proximate cause linking any alleged negligence to the child's injuries.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: failure to give pre-operative antibiotics, unnecessary removal of appendix, failure to tie off appendix stump, failure to diagnose peritonitis, failure to administer post-operative antibiotics
Injury_Type_Secondary: post-operative peritonitis, paralytic ileus, bowel obstruction, bleeding, ulcers, neurological deficits, hepatitis, staphylococcal abscesses, permanent disability
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Government physicians failed to diagnose peritonitis until several days after the initial appendectomy.
Operative_Text_Snippet: The operation disclosed that the boy’s appendix was not completely sound, but that he had not been suffering from appendicitis.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: the boy had some bleeding into the peritoneal cavity which was causing paralytic ileus— a failure of the contractibility of the intestines— with a resulting reduction of the ability of the intestines to pass along gas and solid matter, and subsiding gastroenteritis.
Plaintiff_Demographics: 11-year-old male, son of a military service member, civilian patient at a military hospital.
Preventability_Assessment: Court concluded no negligence and that the injuries were not preventable by the government physicians.

Facility_Type: Silver Cross Hospital (civilian hospital) and prison infirmary

Case_Name: Phyllis Jean Pierce WILSON and William Edgar Wilson, III, Plaintiffs, v. UNITED STATES of America, Defendant.
Court: United States District Court, M.D. Alabama, Northern Division
Expert_Criticism_Text: Defense experts argued that rectal examinations and blood counts would not have reliably diagnosed appendicitis and that the delay in surgery was medically justified.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendant contended that the standard of care did not require the examinations, that diagnosis was uncertain, that the delay was necessary for patient safety, and that no causation between delay and sterility was shown.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: delayed diagnosis of appendicitis, failure to perform rectal examination, failure to obtain blood count, failure to diagnose acute appendicitis timely, failure to perform timely appendectomy, inadequate informed consent
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Ruptured appendix discovered during exploratory laparotomy on October 23, 1971.
Operative_Text_Snippet: An exploratory laparotomy was performed... the doctors found that plaintiff’s appendix had indeed ruptured. ... The presence of wide-scale inflammation ... a small tube was placed ...
Difficulty_Text_Snippet: the abdomen ... was reddened and edematous with multiple adhesions in the area of the uterus.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: During this operation, a surgical decision was made not to remove the ruptured appendix. The presence of wide-scale inflammation and the possibility of bowel damage ... mandated this decision.
Plaintiff_Demographics: young female civilian patient; age not specified; later married; experienced sterility.
Preventability_Assessment: Court concluded that the alleged delays did not constitute negligence and were not the proximate cause of the plaintiff’s sterility.

Plaintiff_Demographics: male inmate, prisoner #497-528, age not stated
Surgeon_Characteristics: Dr. Sherman Katz (professor, OSU Medical Center), Dr. Scott Armen, Dr. Richard Fries, Dr. Irina Pleister (surgeons assisting in appendectomy), Dr. Katz (attending at CMC follow‑up clinic)
Facility_Type: Ohio State University Medical Center (state university hospital) and Corrections Medical Center
Preventability_Assessment: Court concluded plaintiff failed to allege deliberate indifference; ordinary negligence is insufficient for a §1983 claim, thus the alleged injuries were not deemed legally preventable under the constitutional standard.

Case_Name: Roger L. Robbins, Plaintiff-Appellant, v. Franklin M. Lotter, Miss Mary Kimbill, Milwaukee County House of Corrections Medical Staff, and the Milwaukee County Corporation Counsel, Defendants-Appellees
Court: United States Court of Appeals, Seventh Circuit
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendants argued the complaint was frivolous and sought dismissal under 28 U.S.C. §1915(d), asserting the plaintiff's claims lacked merit.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: delayed diagnosis of appendicitis, failure to provide emergency medical care, false imprisonment, cruel and unusual punishment
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Robbins was locked in a steel‑door room for two days before being examined and taken to the hospital.
Operative_Text_Snippet: Doctors found that Robbins’ appendix had developed gangrene.
Plaintiff_Demographics: Male prisoner; age not stated; incarcerated in Milwaukee County House of Corrections

Case_Name: Jeremiah James PETLIG, Plaintiff, v. Scott CARTER-ELDRED, et al., Defendants
Court: United States District Court, W.D. Washington, at Seattle.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendant argued plaintiff’s delay in seeking care and that the officer’s response was reasonable, amounting at most to negligence, not deliberate indifference; also raised qualified immunity which the court declined to consider.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: delayed diagnosis of appendicitis, failure to call code two, failure to initiate medical status two, deliberate indifference
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Plaintiff alleged officer failed to act on his complaints on February 10, leading to diagnosis on February 12.
Operative_Text_Snippet: He received a diagnosis of acute appendicitis and an appendectomy in the early morning of February 13, 2018.
Plaintiff_Demographics: pretrial detainee, male, incarcerated at Clallam Bay Corrections Center, age not stated
Preventability_Assessment: Court concluded the officer’s conduct amounted to negligence, not deliberate indifference, and granted summary judgment for the defense.

Case_Name: David R. ESNOUF, Sr. v. Warden MATTY et al.
Court: United States District Court, E.D. Pennsylvania
Procedural_Posture: Motion to amend and summary judgment
Expert_Criticism_Text: The court criticized the plaintiff's expert report as unsigned, unsworn, not filed of record, and lacking personal knowledge, rendering it ineffective to support a §1983 claim.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendant argues that the nurse was not a state actor because she was employed by an independent contractor, and therefore the §1983 claim fails; the defense moves for summary judgment on that ground.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: negligent diagnosis and treatment, failure to transfer to hospital
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Plaintiff alleges delayed diagnosis and failure to transfer to hospital early enough to prevent appendix rupture.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: Plaintiff alleges that the defendants were negligent in their diagnosis and treatment of his condition and failed to appreciate the seriousness of his condition in time to alert prison officials so that those officials could order a transfer to the hospital early enough to avert the harm.
Plaintiff_Demographics: Inmate; age not specified
Preventability_Assessment: Court found negligence but concluded no deliberate indifference; the harm was deemed preventable under ordinary negligence standards.
Reviewer_Notes: Clinical details such as operative approach, difficulty, and specific injury outcomes are not described in the opinion, leading to many UNKNOWN fields.

Case_Name: JUAN HERNÁNDEZ-SÁNCHEZ, et al., Plaintiffs, v. HOSPITAL SAN CRISTOBAL, et al., Defendants.
Court: United States District Court, D. Puerto Rico.
Expert_Criticism_Text: Court found the expert lacked a medical license, had no surgical experience, failed to meet continuing education requirements, and provided conclusory, unsupported opinions, rendering his testimony unreliable.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendants moved to exclude the plaintiff's expert, arguing lack of qualifications, no admitting privileges, no license, and failure to meet Rule 702 standards.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: delayed diagnosis of appendicitis, failure to stabilize emergency condition, failure to screen under EMTALA
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Surgeon discovered intrabdominal abscess, necrotizing infection, and perforated hollow viscus during exploratory laparotomy.
Operative_Text_Snippet: At 2:45 p.m., the general anesthesia was induced, and the operation began at 3:45 p.m. Dr. Cebollero performed an exploratory laparotomy and found an intrabdominal abscess, necrotizing infection of the abdominal wall and peritonitis. He did a colostomy, appendectomy, gastrostomy, removed the previous PEG, did a debridement of the abdominal wall, and obtained a biopsy of the liver.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: The admission diagnosis was perforated hollow viscus. Dr. Cebollero performed an exploratory laparotomy and found an intrabdominal abscess, necrotizing infection of the abdominal wall and peritonitis.
Plaintiff_Demographics: 68-year-old male, status post PEG placement
Reviewer_Notes: Opinion focuses on expert admissibility; limited clinical detail, many fields uncertain.

Case_Name: Delmart E.J.M. VREELAND, II, Plaintiff, v. Thomas C. FISHER, MD, Defendant.
Court: United States District Court, D. Colorado.
Expert_Criticism_Text: Defense expert Dr. Susan Tiona testified that the bacterial count found was contamination and not clinically significant, indicating no infection.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendant argued plaintiff did not suffer a sufficiently serious medical condition, showing no infection, weight loss, or harm from lack of bandages or pain medication, thus no deliberate indifference.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: deliberate indifference, failure to provide bandages, failure to provide pain medication, failure to treat infection
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Plaintiff alleged Dr. Fisher refused bandages and pain medication after surgery.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: Plaintiff states that, on March 13, 2012, plaintiff told Dr. Fisher that he was “suffering in pain, was swollen, leaking blood and green stuff, and asked for examination, bandages, and pain meds,” but that Dr. Fisher “refused any treatment at all.”
Plaintiff_Demographics: Male, pro se inmate, incarcerated at Buena Vista Correctional Facility, underwent appendectomy in February 2012.
Reviewer_Notes: Many clinical details missing; custody status inferred; procedural details unclear.

Case_Name: Kandie R. WRIGHT, Plaintiff v. David H. SMITH, M.D. and Abingdon Surgical Associates, P.C., Defendants.
Court: United States District Court, W.D. Virginia, Abingdon Division.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendant argues Dr. Smith was not an employee of ASA at the time of treatment, invoking respondeat superior, and asserts plaintiff failed to mitigate damages by not returning for follow‑up and delaying emergency care.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: failure to remove appendix, delayed diagnosis of appendicitis, improper postoperative care, failure to inform patient of pathology results, poor communication
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Pathology report on Feb 23‑24 indicated no appendix was identified, but surgeon took no action.
Operative_Text_Snippet: Dr. Smith began the surgery as a laparoscopic procedure, but due to difficulties with visualization and mobilization, he converted it to an open procedure. Despite this conversion, Dr. Smith continued to have visualization problems, but he proceeded with the surgery, removing tissue that he felt confident contained Wright’s appendix.
Difficulty_Text_Snippet: Dr. Smith began the surgery as a laparoscopic procedure, but due to difficulties with visualization and mobilization, he converted it to an open procedure. Despite this conversion, Dr. Smith continued to have visualization problems.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: The pathological report ... stated that “there is no actual appendix identified in the material examined.”
Plaintiff_Demographics: Female, age not stated, civilian patient.
Surgeon_Characteristics: Dr. David H. Smith, M.D., general surgeon, employed by ASA/APP.

Case_Name: Ervin Charles ST. AMAND, Plaintiff, v. Sherman BLOCK, et al., Defendants.
Court: United States District Court, C.D. California.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendants argued the complaint was unsigned, failed to identify specific defendants and personal involvement, and lacked sufficient factual allegations to state a claim.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: delayed diagnosis of appendicitis, failure to provide medical care, deliberate indifference, failure to change dressing, property deprivation
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Plaintiff alleges he was not seen by a doctor until the evening of September 30, 1999, after a week of denied care.
Operative_Text_Snippet: "appendectomy-laparotomy"
Recognition_Text_Snippet: "Defendants were deliberately indifferent to Plaintiff’s request for medical treatment for a week"
Plaintiff_Demographics: Male, below-the-knee amputee, incarcerated in Los Angeles County Men’s Central Jail, age not stated.
Facility_Type: jail infirmary and LAC/USC Medical Center
Reviewer_Notes: Limited clinical detail; many fields unknown.

Case_Name: John BENEDICT, Plaintiff, v. UNITED STATES of America, Defendant.
Court: United States District Court, E.D. Michigan, Northern Division.
Procedural_Posture: Order denying defendant's motion for summary judgment
Expert_Criticism_Text: Plaintiff's expert Dr. Farber alleged Dr. Harshman breached the standard by failing to recognize appendicitis and not referring to an ER; defense expert argued the patient's symptoms were atypical and not indicative of appendicitis.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendant argues Dr. Harshman did not breach the standard because symptoms were not classic for appendicitis, that the inflammatory mass was unrelated to delayed diagnosis, and that the plaintiff is over 50% at fault for not seeking emergency care.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: failure to recognize symptoms of appendicitis, failure to refer to emergency department, delayed diagnosis of appendicitis
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Dr. Harshman failed to diagnose appendicitis at the VA visit; diagnosis was made after CT at the emergency department.
Operative_Text_Snippet: Dr. Nunoo commenced the surgery at 9:23 PM, but was unable to find Benedict’s appendix laparoscopically due to a large inflammatory mass surrounding the appendix and cecum. He therefore converted to an open procedure with the assistance of another physician, Trung Nguyen. After the conversion, Dr. Nunoo discovered that Benedict’s appendix had ruptured and there was some stool spillage.
Difficulty_Text_Snippet: Unable to find the appendix laparoscopically due to a large inflammatory mass surrounding the appendix and cecum, requiring conversion to open surgery.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: The CT Scan was performed at 6:51 PM, and was “consistent with acute appendicitis.”
Plaintiff_Demographics: Veteran male; age not specified; civilian patient at VA clinic.
Preventability_Assessment: Experts testified that timely diagnosis and referral could have prevented appendix rupture, ileocecectomy, and subsequent complications.
